perturbation.original_text: 1.1 “Invention”, the Pipeline Piranha Invention, Patent Pending #103573330A, shall mean all information relating to products, applications, systems, components, technologies and business topics. Including but not limited to prototypes, drawings, data, trade secrets and in tellectual property relating to the “Patent Pending” invention named Pipeline Piranha.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 “Invention”, the Pipeline Piranha Invention, Patent Pending #103573330A, shall mean some information relating to products, applications, systems, components, technologies and business topics. Including but not limited to prototypes, drawings, data, trade secrets and in tellectual property relating to the “Patent Pending” invention named Pipeline Piranha.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'all information' to 'some information', we create ambiguity. Trade secrets law requires specific identification and protection of the trade secret. The change introduces vagueness that might hinder enforcement under trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORSA 646.461 defines trade secrets and requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. By changing 'all information' to 'some information', the agreement now fails to clearly define what is protected, potentially voiding trade secret protection because the agreement does not specify all information which needs protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ORS 646.461 – Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475 ORS 646.461 Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475 Text Annotations As used in ORS 646.461 (Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475) to 646.475 (Application and construction of ORS 646.461 to 646.475) , unless the context otherwise requires: (1) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering and independent development alone shall not be considered improper means. (2) “Misappropriation” means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (c) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who, before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake; or (d) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person, who at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (3) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) “Trade secret” means information, including a drawing, cost data, customer list, formula, pattern, compilation, pro
perturbation.justification: The change from 'all information' to 'some information' creates ambiguity, potentially voiding trade secret protection. ORSA 646.461, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines 'trade secret' as 'information,' implying a specific and comprehensive scope. The modified text weakens this definition by not specifying *all* the information subject to protection, thus failing to meet the standard of clearly defining what constitutes the trade secret. This misalignment directly contradicts the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as the protected information's scope is now unclear, making enforcement difficult.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information” shall mean all information provided by Disclosing Party with respect to the Invention regardless of whether it is written, oral, audio tapes, video tapes, computer disc s, machines, prototypes, designs, specifications, articles of manufacture, drawings, huma n or machine readable documents. Confidential Information shall also include all information related to the Invention provided by Disclosing Pa rty to Receiving Party prior to the signing Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of this agreement. Confidential Information shall not include any information in the public domain at the time of the disclosure, or subsequently comes within the public domain without fault of the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information” shall mean information provided by Disclosing Party with respect to the Invention regardless of whether it is written, oral, audio tapes, video tapes, computer disc s, machines, prototypes, designs, specifications, articles of manufacture, drawings, huma n or machine readable documents. Confidential Information shall also include information related to the Invention provided by Disclosing Pa rty to Receiving Party prior to the signing Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of this agreement. Confidential Information shall not include any information in the public domain at the time of the disclosure, or subsequently comes within the public domain.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'without fault of the Receiving Party' regarding information entering the public domain, we shift the burden of responsibility. Even if the Receiving Party causes information to enter the public domain, it's no longer considered a breach, weakening confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted by Oregon, ORSA 646.461)
perturbation.law_explanation: ORSA 646.461(2)(b) implicitly assumes that misappropriation (e.g., causing a trade secret to enter the public domain) must be wrongful. Removing the 'without fault' clause suggests that even if the Receiving Party negligently or intentionally causes the information to become public, it's no longer confidential. This contradicts the purpose of trade secret law, which is to prevent improper use or disclosure.

perturbation.justification: The original contract holds the Receiving Party accountable if they are at fault for confidential information entering the public domain. The modified contract eliminates this accountability, contradicting the aim of trade secret law. As the Law Explanation states, ORSA 646.461(2)(b) implicitly assumes that misappropriation must be wrongful. By removing 'without fault of the Receiving Party,' the modified contract suggests that even if the Receiving Party negligently or intentionally causes the information to become public, it's no longer a breach, which subverts the protection afforded to trade secrets under Oregon law as outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 1, Chapter 646.

perturbation.original_text: 5. GOVERNING LAW This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement (including, without limitation, provisions concerning limitations of actions), shall be governed by and cons trued in accordance with the laws of the State Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 4 of Oregon, notwithstanding any conflict-of -laws doctrines of such state or other jurisdiction to the contrary, and without th e aid of any canon, custom or rule of law requiring construction against the draftsman.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. GOVERNING LAW This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement (including, without limitation, provisions concerning limitations of actions), shall be governed by and cons trued in accordance with the laws of the State Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 4 of Oregon, notwithstanding any conflict-of -laws doctrines of such state or other jurisdiction to the contrary, and with th e aid of any canon, custom or rule of law requiring construction against the draftsman.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'without the aid' to 'with the aid', we reverse the instruction on how to interpret the contract. Oregon law generally disfavors construing contracts against the drafter, but this modification mandates doing so, creating a conflict with established principles of contract interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Oregon contract law principles of interpretation
perturbation.law_citation: State of Oregon Court Decisions Regarding Contract Interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: Oregon courts generally interpret contracts to give effect to the parties' intent, and do not automatically construe ambiguities against the drafter, especially when both parties are sophisticated. This change directly contradicts this established practice, potentially leading a court to misinterpret the parties' intentions.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Oregon Supreme Court Decisions :: Oregon Case Law :: Oregon Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Oregon Supreme Court Decisions The Oregon Supreme Court consists of seven justices. It conducts a direct review of decisions in death penalty cases, Oregon Tax Court cases, certain agency proceedings, and cases involving certain labor law injunctions. The Court also has the discretion to review any decisions by the Court of Appeals, as well as questions certified by the Court of Appeals, a court of another state, or a federal court. It has original jurisdiction over issuing writs such as habeas corpus and mandamus, in addition to certain matters related to elections. The Court oversees the practice of law throughout the state, and it may conduct disciplinary proceedings related to the fitness of a judge or attorney. Each justice on the Oregon Supreme Court serves a term of six years. Justices are chosen in non-partisan elections, so voters do not see the party affiliation of a candidate on the ballot. However, the Governor of Oregon holds the authority to appoint a justice if a vacant seat arises in the middle of a term. The interim justice must run in the next general election if they seek to remain on the court for a full term. Oregon imposes a mandatory retirement age of 75 on justices. A candidate for this position must be a U.S. citizen and a resident of Oregon for at least three years before being appointed or elected. They also must have been admitted to practice law in the Oregon Supreme Court. The Chief Justice of the Court is chosen by the other justices and holds that position for the full six years. They supervise the administrative and financial tasks related to running the state judiciary. A justice may face removal in certain situations. These include a felony conviction, a violation of a rule of judicial conduct, incompetence to perform the duties of their position, and alcohol or drug addiction. perturbation.justification: The modification changes the contract's interpretation by instructing it to be construed 'with the aid of any canon, custom or rule of law requiring construction against the draftsman,' which directly opposes the original instruction and conflicts with general principles of contract interpretation, potentially leading to misinterpretation of the parties' intentions, as the Law Explanation indicates. While the scraped snippets don't directly address this specific point, the change undermines established practices for contract interpretation, as the Law Explanation indicates. This creates a legal contradiction because the altered clause mandates a construction approach that is generally disfavored.

perturbation.original_text: 10. ARBITRATION Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by arbitration conducted by the Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Asso ciation and in accordance with the rules thereof, or in any other convenient forum agreed to in writing by the parties. Any arbitration award shall be final and binding, and judgment upon the award rendered pursuant to such arbitration may be entered in any court of proper jurisdiction. Notwithstanding the forego ing, either party may seek and obtain temporary injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. ARBITRATION Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by arbitration conducted by the Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Asso ciation and in accordance with the rules thereof, or in any other convenient forum agreed to in writing by the parties. Any arbitration award shall be advisory only, and judgment upon the award rendered pursuant to such arbitration may be persuasive but not binding. Notwithstanding the forego ing, either party may seek and obtain temporary injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'final and binding' to 'advisory only', the arbitration clause becomes non-binding. This undermines the purpose of arbitration, which is to provide a conclusive resolution. This potentially conflicts with the Federal Arbitration Act, which favors enforcement of arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S.C. § 2 states that arbitration agreements shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. By stipulating that the arbitration award is 'advisory only,' the agreement is rendered unenforceable, directly contradicting the FAA's intent to make arbitration agreements binding.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts 9 U.S.C. § 2, which mandates that arbitration agreements be 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable.' By changing the arbitration award to be 'advisory only' and 'persuasive but not binding,' the changed text renders the arbitration agreement unenforceable, thus contradicting the FAA as stated in 9 U.S. Code § 2: 'Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate'.
